INTRODUCTION - Pericision is a minor surgical procedure that is
undertaken to counter the relapse tendancy of
the stretched gengival fibres

Also known as - sulas slice procedure
- Edwards procedure
(3MPQ)
PRINCIPLE - The main reason for relapse after orthod ontic treatment
IS re bound of the network of elastic supracrestal
gingival fibres
- -when the reath move to a new position these fibres
are stretched and remodelling of these fibres takes a
long time
- Pericision involves elimination of the pull of elastic
supracrestal gingival fibres by sectioning these Fibres
-Teethare held in corrected position when the fibres heal,
thereby reducing the relapse caused by elasticity of
gingival fibres

Cleft lip d palate Pag 183 of

Cleft up and palate are congenilat abnormalities that affect the upper
lip and the hard and soft palate of the mouth

severity may range from a small notch in the up to a complete fissure(groove)
extending into the roof of the mouth and nose

INCIDENCE 1 in every 600 - 1000births
- Cleft lip is common among males while deft palate
is more common among females

on either side of the stomodeum [future oralcavity] is the developing
mandebular arch, the dorsal end of which gives off a bud called
the masellary process
with the formation of the nasal pits, the frontonasal process gels
divided into a medial nasal process and 2 lateral nasal process

Upper lip and primary palate is thus formed by the fusion of the
maxillary process with the medial and lateral nasal processis

The upper up and the premary palate usually start fusing by the
week of gestation

Failure of these developmental processes may lead to unilateral
or bilateral defect along the lines of normal fusion in the lip or palate

by Drillen reported that in 3 children
with clefts had some relatives with similar congenital
defects
- clefts of lip and palate can be transmitted as a dominant
or recessive trait

TGFB-3, AP2, MSXI are genes that have been
identified as having a major role in the development
of deft lip and palale
- If 1 parent has a cleft deformity, there is 3

2% chance
that there first born will have a cleft lip of palate

Pericision [circum ferential supra - crestal fibrotomy]
[3M PQ] /[5MPQ]

INTRODUCTION - Pericision is a minor surgical procedure that is
undertaken to counter the relapse tendancy of
the stretched gengival fibres.
- . Also known as - sulas slice procedure
- Edwards procedure
(3MPQ)
PRINCIPLE - The main reason for relapse after orthod ontic treatment
IS re bound of the network of elastic supracrestal
gingival fibres
- -when the reath move to a new position these fibres
are stretched and remodelling of these fibres takes a
long time
- Pericision involves elimination of the pull of elastic
supracrestal gingival fibres by sectioning these Fibres
-Teethare held in corrected position when the fibres heal,
thereby reducing the relapse caused by elasticity of
gingival fibres
PROCEDURE - Under local anesthesia, the sharp point of the fine blade
[NO .15 BP blade] is inserted In to the gingival crevice up
to the alveolars crest of the bone
- The blade is kept paeallel to the tooth surface
- Blade is passed around the circumference of the
tooth
this severs the fibres connecting tooth to
the gingival soft hssues.
- cuts are made enterproxumally on each side of the
rotated tooth.

- cutsare made along the labial and lingual gengival
margers also. IF labial gingivals this, the cutis elimenated
- Pencision IS raken as an adjunctive retention procedure
after the correction of rotations.
orthognathic surgery
INTRODUCTION - Moderate to severa occlusal disprepances and
dentofacial deformities in late adolescents and
adults usually require combined orthodontic
treatment and orthognathic surgeries to obtain
optimum results.

long_essay.question: Write a short note on THEOREMS ON RETENTION.
long_essay.model_answer:
subaofiompa
5MPQ - Enumerate various theorms on retenhon. Adda note relentum 266
Riedel has summarized the different concepts and phelosophics into
9 theorems. To this list Moyer adeded the 10th therem
D THEOREM Teeth that have been moved tend to return to their
former position'
- The reasons for the teeth to go back to their original position
enclude apicalbase, transseptal fetres Imusculature
- This thureno is true for rolation
2) THEOREM 2:- Elimination of cause of malocclusum will prevent relapse'
-This theorem is true for malocelusum with known ehologic
factors like thumbsucking, tongue thrusting etc.
- If the cause of malocelusion is known every step should betaken
to remove or eleminate the etrology for malocelusun
3) THEOREM 3: 'Malocelusion should be over corrected as a safety factor'
- The concept of overcerrecting is to allow or expect some amount
of relapse 80 that after relapse there occlusion is normal.
- It is done when treating class ", class malocelusion rotations
4) THEOREM 4:- Proper occlusion is a potent factor in holding teeth in their
corrected positions'
- correct intercuspation is an essential factor in occlusal stabilization
and equally emp is to obtain proper functional occlusion
5) THEOREM 'Bone and adjacent tissues must be allowed to reorganize
around newly positioned teeth'
- when teeth are moved orthodontically, numerous changes ocer in
the bone and surrounding tessue
- It lakes considerable time for organization to be completed.
- The new osteoid bone formed around recently moved teeth
offers enadequate retention.
- Retentun appleances are based on this principle
6) THEOREM 6:- If the lower encesors are placed upright over basal bone
they are more likely to remain is good alignment'
- Treatment should be aimed to place the lower inuses in proper
angulation and position
- stability is better if the encisors are placed upright over basalbone
7) THEOREM 7 :- 'Corrections carried out during periods of growth are less
likely to relapse

- Treatment carried out during growth allows the tessue to adjust the
better' i., Orthodontic treatment should be instituted at
earleest possible age
- Early treatment planning offers greater malocelusion. advantage in longterm
slability; partecularly in skeletal

8) THEOREM :- Arch form particularly in the mandibular arch, can't be

allowed permanently by appliance therapy.'
- Alteratun of existing archforms results in increased risk of relapse.
- Treatment should be aimed at maintaining the arch form presented
by the malocelusion as much as possible
- The intereanine width in the mandible represents muscular balance
P dictates the limite of dental expansion
9) THEOREM ' The farther the teeth have been moved, the less likelihood
of relapse'.
- The farther the looth hasbeen moved the lesser is the risk of it
relitening to its original position
- It is better to minimire extensive tooth movement by interceptive
procedures like guidance of eruption, use of orthopaedic ferces &
functional appliance

short_answer.model_answer:
Given in detail in interceptive orthodontics chapleage 27 of 266
Extraction of supernumerary, impacted Dankylosed teeth
Presenceof supernumerary /impacted/ankylosed reeth are important
local cause of malocclusion.
Prior to extraction of these teeth, theirexact location and their
relationship radiographs - with adjacent structures should be ascertained by
Tooth location. is approached by buccal 1 palatal flap depending uponits
After careful clevation of the flap, adequate amount of bone is

The impacted or supernumerary toothis removed and the extraction
socket inspected for any pathological tissue
The flapis repositioned and sutures placed for a week.

Pericision [circum ferential supra - crestal fibrotomy]
[3M PQ] /[5MPQ]

INTRODUCTION - Pericision is a minor surgical procedure that is
undertaken to counter the relapse tendancy of
the stretched gengival fibres.
- . Also known as - sulas slice procedure
- Edwards procedure
(3MPQ)
PRINCIPLE - The main reason for relapse after orthod ontic treatment
IS re bound of the network of elastic supracrestal
gingival fibres
- -when the reath move to a new position these fibres
are stretched and remodelling of these fibres takes a
long time
- Pericision involves elimination of the pull of elastic
supracrestal gingival fibres by sectioning these Fibres
-Teethare held in corrected position when the fibres heal,
thereby reducing the relapse caused by elasticity of
gingival fibres
PROCEDURE - Under local anesthesia, the sharp point of the fine blade
[NO .15 BP blade] is inserted In to the gingival crevice up
to the alveolars crest of the bone
- The blade is kept paeallel to the tooth surface
- Blade is passed around the circumference of the
tooth
this severs the fibres connecting tooth to
the gingival soft hssues.
- cuts are made enterproxumally on each side of the
rotated tooth.

- cutsare made along the labial and lingual gengival
margers also. IF labial gingivals this, the cutis elimenated
- Pencision IS raken as an adjunctive retention procedure
after the correction of rotations.
orthognathic surgery
INTRODUCTION - Moderate to severa occlusal disprepances and
dentofacial deformities in late adolescents and
adults usually require combined orthodontic
treatment and orthognathic surgeries to obtain
optimum results.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Enlow's V' principle.
short_answer.model_answer:
Many facial bone or pasts of bone have a 'V'shaped pattern of growth.
The growth movements and enlargement of these bones occur towards the
wide ends of the'v' as a result of differential deposition and selective resorption
of bone
Bone deposition occurs on the innerside of the wide end of the 'v'and bone
reserption on the outer surface
Deposition also takes place at the ends of the 2arms of the 'v'resulting is
growth movement towards the ends.
The'v' pattern of the growth occurs in a number of regions such as the
base of the mandible, ends of long bones, mandibular body, palate etc.

- centre of resistance of a tooth can be defined as that point on the

body moves equally in the direction of force applied.

tooth, crest it lies b/w one third and one half of the root, apical toolvelar
- In multirooted tooth, the centre of resistance lies b/w the roots, 1-2mm
apical to the furcation.

Cleft lip d palate Pag 183 of

Cleft up and palate are congenilat abnormalities that affect the upper
lip and the hard and soft palate of the mouth.
severity may range from a small notch in the up to a complete fissure(groove)
extending into the roof of the mouth and nose.
INCIDENCE 1 in every 600 - 1000births
- Cleft lip is common among males while deft palate
is more common among females.
- unilateral cleft
80%
bilateral cleft
itolog
20%

short_answer.question: Write a short note on EMBRYOLOGY.
short_answer.model_answer:
on either side of the stomodeum [future oralcavity] is the developing
mandebular arch, the dorsal end of which gives off a bud called
the masellary process
with the formation of the nasal pits, the frontonasal process gels
divided into a medial nasal process and 2 lateral nasal process.
Upper lip and primary palate is thus formed by the fusion of the
maxillary process with the medial and lateral nasal processis.
The upper up and the premary palate usually start fusing by the
week of gestation.
Failure of these developmental processes may lead to unilateral
or bilateral defect along the lines of normal fusion in the lip or palate

short_answer.question: Write a short note on I HEREDITARY.
short_answer.model_answer:
- A study conducted. by Drillen reported that in 3 children
with clefts had some relatives with similar congenital
defects
- clefts of lip and palate can be transmitted as a dominant
or recessive trait.
- TGFA; TGFB-3, AP2, MSXI are genes that have been
identified as having a major role in the development
of deft lip and palale.
- If 1 parent has a cleft deformity, there is 3.2% chance
that there first born will have a cleft lip of palate.

MATERNAL INFECTION AND TOXICITY, DIETARY IMBALANCE
- infections - rubella, influenza
- Toxicity. - hypoxia, let teratogenic drugs like valium,
phenytoin, corticosteroids, cytotoxic drugs

short_answer.question: Write a short note on The important landmarks are.
short_answer.model_answer:
1> NASION - The most anterior point midway between the frontal
and nasal bones on the fronto-nasal suture.
2> ORBITALE - The lowest point on the inferior bony margin of arbit
3> PORION - The highest bony point on the upper margin of external
acoustic meatis.
4> SELLA - The point representing the midpoint of the pituitary
fassa or sella tursica
5> POINT A - It is the deepest point in the midline between anterior
nasal spine and alveolar crest between 2 central incisors.
It is also called as subspinale.
6> POINT B- It is the deepest point in the midline between the
alveolar crest of mandible and the mental. process.
It is also called supramentale.
7> BASION - It is the median point of the anterior margin of foramen
magnum.
TATEOUAH930
8> BOLTON POINT - The highest point at the past condylar notchof
the occipital bone
g> ANTERIOR NASAL SPINE - It is the anterior tip of the sharp bony
process of the maxilla in the midline of the lower margin
of anterior nasal spine.
10> GONION - It is a constructed point at the junction of ramal plane
and the mandibular plane.
11> POGONION - It is the most anterior point' of the bony chin in the
median plane
12> MENTON - It is the most inferior midline point on the mandibular
symphysis.
13> GNATHION - It is the most antero inferior point on the symphysis
of the chin. It is drawn by intersecting a Line drawn perpendicul-
-ar to the line connecting menton and pogonion.
14> ARTICULARE - It is a point at the junction of the posterior barder
of ramus and the inferior border of the basilar part of the
occipital bone.
15> CONDYLION - The most superior point on the head of condyle.
16> PROSTHION - The lowest most anteriors Dointy on that alveolar bone
in the midline, between the upper central incisors.
It is also called supradentale.
17> INFRADENTALE - The highest and most anterior point ЗИА on the
alveolar process, in the median plane between the mandibular
central incisors.
18> KEY RIDGE - The lower most point on the contour of the anterior
wall of infratemporal fossa.
19> POSTERIOR NASAL SPINE - The intersection of a continuation of
the anterior wall of the pterygopalatine fassa and the floor of
the nase.
20> PTM POINT - It is the intersection of the inferior barder of faramen
rotundum with the pasterior wall of Terygo-maxillary fissure.
21> GLABELLA - It is the most prominent point of the farhead in the
mid-sagittal plane.
22> CHELION - It is the Lateral terminus of the oral slit on the outer
corner of mouth.
23> SUBNASALE - The point where the lowest barder of the nase meets
the outer contour of the upper lip.

Pericision [circum ferential supra - crestal fibrotomy]
[3M PQ] /[5MPQ]

INTRODUCTION - Pericision is a minor surgical procedure that is
undertaken to counter the relapse tendancy of
the stretched gengival fibres.
- . Also known as - sulas slice procedure
- Edwards procedure
(3MPQ)
PRINCIPLE - The main reason for relapse after orthod ontic treatment
IS re bound of the network of elastic supracrestal
gingival fibres
- -when the reath move to a new position these fibres
are stretched and remodelling of these fibres takes a
long time
- Pericision involves elimination of the pull of elastic
supracrestal gingival fibres by sectioning these Fibres
-Teethare held in corrected position when the fibres heal,
thereby reducing the relapse caused by elasticity of
gingival fibres
PROCEDURE - Under local anesthesia, the sharp point of the fine blade
[NO .15 BP blade] is inserted In to the gingival crevice up
to the alveolars crest of the bone
- The blade is kept paeallel to the tooth surface
- Blade is passed around the circumference of the
tooth
this severs the fibres connecting tooth to
the gingival soft hssues.
- cuts are made enterproxumally on each side of the
rotated tooth.

- cutsare made along the labial and lingual gengival
margers also. IF labial gingivals this, the cutis elimenated
- Pencision IS raken as an adjunctive retention procedure
after the correction of rotations.

(I) SCAMMON'S CURVE OF GROWTH [5/3MPQ]
Human body comprises of 4 major hssue- Lymphoid tissue

- General tissue
- Geniral tissue.
Each of these tessues grows at diff rates and difftimes.

viva.question: Write a short note on Enlow's V' principle.
viva.model_answer:
Many facial bone or pasts of bone have a 'V'shaped pattern of growth.
The growth movements and enlargement of these bones occur towards the
wide ends of the'v' as a result of differential deposition and selective resorption
of bone
Bone deposition occurs on the innerside of the wide end of the 'v'and bone
reserption on the outer surface
Deposition also takes place at the ends of the 2arms of the 'v'resulting is
growth movement towards the ends.
The'v' pattern of the growth occurs in a number of regions such as the
base of the mandible, ends of long bones, mandibular body, palate etc.

3> PORION - The highest bony point on the upper margin of external

4> SELLA - The point representing the midpoint of the pituitary

5> POINT A - It is the deepest point in the midline between anterior
nasal spine and alveolar crest between 2 central incisors.
It is also called as subspinale.
6> POINT B- It is the deepest point in the midline between the
alveolar crest of mandible and the mental. process.
It is also called supramentale.
7> BASION - It is the median point of the anterior margin of foramen

8> BOLTON POINT - The highest point at the past condylar notchof

g> ANTERIOR NASAL SPINE - It is the anterior tip of the sharp bony
process of the maxilla in the midline of the lower margin
of anterior nasal spine.
10> GONION - It is a constructed point at the junction of ramal plane
and the mandibular plane.
11> POGONION - It is the most anterior point' of the bony chin in the
median plane
12> MENTON - It is the most inferior midline point on the mandibular
symphysis.
13> GNATHION - It is the most antero inferior point on the symphysis
of the chin. It is drawn by intersecting a Line drawn perpendicul-
-ar to the line connecting menton and pogonion.
14> ARTICULARE - It is a point at the junction of the posterior barder
of ramus and the inferior border of the basilar part of the
occipital bone.
15> CONDYLION - The most superior point on the head of condyle.
16> PROSTHION - The lowest most anteriors Dointy on that alveolar bone
in the midline, between the upper central incisors.
It is also called supradentale.
17> INFRADENTALE - The highest and most anterior point ЗИА on the
alveolar process, in the median plane between the mandibular
central incisors.
18> KEY RIDGE - The lower most point on the contour of the anterior
wall of infratemporal fossa.
19> POSTERIOR NASAL SPINE - The intersection of a continuation of
the anterior wall of the pterygopalatine fassa and the floor of
the nase.
20> PTM POINT - It is the intersection of the inferior barder of faramen
rotundum with the pasterior wall of Terygo-maxillary fissure.
21> GLABELLA - It is the most prominent point of the farhead in the
mid-sagittal plane.
22> CHELION - It is the Lateral terminus of the oral slit on the outer
corner of mouth.
23> SUBNASALE - The point where the lowest barder of the nase meets
the outer contour of the upper lip.

a line joining nasion to point A.
Average value 82°

- A larger than normal value indicates maxilla is
prognothic [class II]

B> S.N.B ANGLE - It is the angle between S.N plane and a line joining

Average value 80°
Significance - it indicates antero-pasterior pasitioning of mandible in

STEINER 1930 ANALYSIS

- Less than normal angle suggests retrusive mandible
